affordances.name: Bounded stress learning design
affordances.mechanism: Convert unavoidable volatility into learning by containing downside, exposing the system to bounded variation, experiments, small failures, and direct feedback, then changing the design so it improves rather than merely survives stress.

use_when: A complex, changing, or adversarial system faces unavoidable volatility and can learn, adapt, or improve from bounded stress.
Downside can be contained while variation, feedback, experiments, or small failures create useful information or stronger designs.
Internal forecasting is too brittle for the environment, so the safer move is to gain from disorder through testing and feedback.
Known unknowns need de-risking before full commitment, and real-world data can steer the next version of the plan.
do_not_use_when: The proposed stress exposure could be ruinous or too large for the system to survive or learn from.
The goal is only to resist shocks, maintain the current state, or survive failure; that is resilience or robustness, not antifragility.
The team is substituting familiar frameworks for a genuinely novel structure instead of testing the situation directly.
Analysis perfectionism is stalling the bounded experiments or feedback loops that would actually teach the system.
case_evidence_needed: The system, strategy, product, process, or organization exposed to volatility, randomness, disorder, or stressors.
The bounded stressors, experiments, small failures, or unexpected inputs that could produce useful information without catastrophic loss.
The anti-goal, worst-case scenario, or downside boundary that must be avoided before exposure is allowed.
The known unknowns to de-risk, the feedback generated by direct engagement, and the comparison method for test outcomes.
The design, assumption, scope, or commitment change that will follow if feedback shows the system is or is not getting better.

treatment_requirements.description: Before treating volatility as useful, define the anti-goal and contain downside so stressors remain survivable and capable of producing learning.
evidence_required: The proposed stressor, experiment, variation, or shock exposure.
The anti-goal or worst-case scenario that would make the exposure unacceptable.
The concrete boundary that keeps the system out of ruinous shock territory.
Evidence that the system can survive, learn from, and adapt after the exposure.
good_output_shape: States the anti-goal before recommending exposure to stress.
Separates survivable stress from ruinous shock.
Explains what downside is contained and how.
Rejects volatility-seeking when no learning or survivability mechanism is present.

treatment_requirements.description: Use a portfolio of bounded tests, direct engagement, and feedback comparison so reality steers the design instead of a single brittle forecast.
evidence_required: The set of possibilities, variants, or hypotheses to test.
The real-world environment or direct engagement point where feedback will be generated.
The outcome comparison method that will show which possibility improved the design.
The next design or strategy update triggered by the feedback.
good_output_shape: Turns a prediction problem into bounded tests where possible.
Specifies how outcomes will be compared.
Names the feedback source and the update it will drive.
Shows how the system gets stronger or smarter after the test rather than merely avoiding loss.

treatment_requirements.description: Convert acknowledged uncertainty into usable knowledge before full commitment by designing de-risking exercises that turn known unknowns into known knowns.
evidence_required: The known unknowns whose resolution would materially change the decision or design.
The de-risking exercise, simulation, test, or feedback source that can resolve each uncertainty.
The surprise or commitment risk reduced by resolving the unknown.
The decision, scope, design, or commitment change made after the unknown is resolved.
good_output_shape: Names the known unknowns instead of leaving them as generic uncertainty.
Attaches each important unknown to a de-risking exercise.
Explains how the exercise changes commitment timing or scale.
Treats de-risking as learning for improvement, not as a static risk register.

diagnostic_questions: Which bounded stressors or small failures can this system survive and learn from, and which shocks would be ruinous?
What would need to be true for this idea to work?
Which known unknown must be de-risked into a known known before full commitment?
What feedback from direct engagement will tell us the design is getting better under stress rather than merely surviving?
Are we trying to predict volatility away, or can we structure the system to gain useful information or strength from it?
misuse_guards: Do not romanticize volatility; antifragility requires survivable exposure and learning capacity, not shocks that can destroy the system.
Do not relabel resilience as antifragility; maintaining state under stress is not the same as improving from stress.
Do not substitute a familiar framework for a genuinely novel situation when direct testing and feedback are needed.
Do not let perfectionist analysis block bounded experiments; the model requires contact with reality and iterative adjustment.
Do not turn de-risking into a generic risk inventory; the output must reduce uncertainty or improve the design before scale.

source_evidence.source_quote: The concept of Antifragility, coined by Nassim Nicholas Taleb, describes a category of things that actively **benefit from shocks**.

Its **fundamental essence** is thriving and growing when exposed to **volatility, randomness, disorder, and stressors**; the antifragile loves adventure, risk, and uncertainty. The purpose of identifying and cultivating antifragility is to move beyond mere survival in a complex world.
**Resilient** or **Robust** items resist shocks and maintain their state.
**Antifragile** items, conversely, improve and **get better** when stressed or hit by disorder.
Antifragility suggests that systems should be designed to benefit from errors and unexpected inputs.

Applying Antifragility in the real world means actively seeking out stressors that lead to beneficial outcomes and structuring decision-making processes to leverage uncertainty, rather than being paralyzed by it.
Instead of aiming directly for a single, aggressive "direct goal," the antifragile approach focuses on defining and **avoiding the anti-goal** or the worst-case scenario.
Rather than trying to predict market winners, adopt a **portfolio of experiments** approach. **Test every single possibility** and systematically compare outcomes.
This continuous validation process helps build conviction and allows real-world data to steer strategy, minimizing uncertainty and risk before full commitment.
Performing **de-risking exercises** transforms these uncertainties into "known knowns," mitigating surprise and giving more complete knowledge of a situation.
This approach requires a **dynamic and iterative** mindset, continually assessing and tweaking the plan.
Antifragility is most powerful in **complex, rapidly changing, and adversarial environments** where volatility is the norm.
**Most useful when** the system can learn, adapt, or improve through bounded stress rather than merely survive it.
**Works best when** downside can be contained while variation, feedback, and small failures create useful information or stronger designs.
**Best used when** uncertainty is unavoidable and the smarter move is to gain from disorder instead of trying to predict it away.
**Danger when** people romanticize volatility and expose the system to ruinous shocks that are too large to survive or learn from.
Using familiar frameworks or mental models that have worked previously but are unhelpful or misleading in a genuinely novel context is a significant anti-pattern (false pattern recognition).
The goal is not to analyze *everything* to achieve perfection, which uses up biological energy and stymies action.

review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed anti-goal work, portfolio testing, de-risking known unknowns, and direct feedback into one operational affordance because the source presents them as parts of the same mechanism: bounded stress exposure that makes the system stronger or smarter.
dropped_material: Did not split inverse goals into a standalone affordance because that would blur into inversion, premortem, and risk-assessment; here it functions as the downside boundary for antifragile exposure.
Did not split experimentation into a standalone affordance because the source uses experiments as the learning vehicle for gaining from volatility, not as a separate model-use contract.
Did not promote systems-thinking, design-thinking, first-principles-thinking, confirmation-bias, curse-of-knowledge, availability-heuristic, or anchoring relation material into separate affordances.
open_questions: Reviewer may later decide whether disruptive-idea evaluation around "What would need to be true for this idea to work?" should remain a diagnostic question inside this affordance or move to an experimentation/first-principles relation layer.

absence_records.reason: The source explicitly distinguishes antifragility from resilience and robustness: maintaining state under shock is not the model's operational affordance because antifragility requires improvement under stress.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Resilient** or **Robust** items resist shocks and maintain their state.
**Antifragile** items, conversely, improve and **get better** when stressed or hit by disorder.

absence_records.reason: The source supports stress exposure only when bounded and learnable; it directly warns against romanticizing volatility or exposing the system to ruinous shocks.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Works best when** downside can be contained while variation, feedback, and small failures create useful information or stronger designs.
**Danger when** people romanticize volatility and expose the system to ruinous shocks that are too large to survive or learn from.

absence_records.reason: The source's anti-goal and de-risking material should not be promoted into a separate generic risk inventory or bounded-wager record. Those boundaries belong to risk-assessment and calculated-risk-taking; antifragility keeps the improving-through-stress mechanism primary.

source_evidence.source_quote: Instead of aiming directly for a single, aggressive "direct goal," the antifragile approach focuses on defining and **avoiding the anti-goal** or the worst-case scenario.
Performing **de-risking exercises** transforms these uncertainties into "known knowns," mitigating surprise and giving more complete knowledge of a situation.
**Most useful when** the system can learn, adapt, or improve through bounded stress rather than merely survive it.
The concept of Antifragility, coined by Nassim Nicholas Taleb, describes a category of things that actively **benefit from shocks**.
**Most useful when** the system can learn, adapt, or improve through bounded stress rather than merely survive it.
**Works best when** downside can be contained while variation, feedback, and small failures create useful information or stronger designs.
Rather than trying to predict market winners, adopt a **portfolio of experiments** approach. **Test every single possibility** and systematically compare outcomes.
**Danger when** people romanticize volatility and expose the system to ruinous shocks that are too large to survive or learn from.

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the extraction contract, schema, Batch 2 selection guidance, full Antifragility Markdown source, activation curation, intervention curation, relation curation, and Batch 1 risk-assessment and calculated-risk-taking boundary records. Extracted one sharp affordance: improving through bounded stress via downside containment, experiments, de-risking, and feedback.
dropped_material: Did not extract a generic risk-response affordance; antifragility is kept on improvement under stress, while risk-assessment handles downside governance and calculated-risk-taking handles pressure-tested bounded wagers.
Did not extract resilience, robustness, or mere survival because the source defines those as contrast concepts rather than antifragility.

Did not extract a third affordance; the source-backed anti-goal, testing, de-risking, and feedback moves are structurally coupled, and splitting them would create generic overlap with inversion, experimentation, and risk-assessment.
Did not compile or modify manifests, source Markdown, Batch 1 records, pilot records, or any other Batch 2 records.
open_questions: Reviewer may want to check whether the de-risking treatment should later cross-link to experimentation rather than remaining inside this antifragility affordance.
